diagrams.title: Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange Protocol
diagrams.description: Step-by-step visualization of how Alice and Bob establish a shared secret without prior shared information
elements: Alice's Private Key: a (random)
Bob's Private Key: b (random)
Public Parameters: p (prime), g (generator)
Alice computes: A = g^a mod p
Bob computes: B = g^b mod p
Alice sends A to Bob over insecure channel
Bob sends B to Alice over insecure channel
Alice computes: K = B^a mod p = (g^b)^a = g^ab mod p
Bob computes: K = A^b mod p = (g^a)^b = g^ab mod p
Both derive identical shared secret K
diagrams.purpose: Illustrate the fundamental DH protocol flow and verify both parties compute the same secret

diagrams.title: Man-in-the-Middle Attack on Diffie-Hellman
diagrams.description: Visualization of how an attacker intercepts and modifies public values to establish separate shared secrets
elements: Alice computes A = g^a, sends to Bob
Mallory intercepts A, replaces with g^x
Bob receives g^x, computes B = g^b, sends to Bob
Mallory intercepts B, replaces with g^y
Alice receives g^y, computes K1 = (g^y)^a = g^ay
Bob computes K2 = (g^x)^b = g^bx
Mallory computes K1 = g^ay and K2 = g^bx
Alice and Bob think they share K1 and K2 respectively, but both share secrets with Mallory
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrate vulnerability to MITM attack and need for authentication

diagrams.title: Discrete Logarithm Problem Visualization
diagrams.description: One-way function visualization showing why modular exponentiation is easy forward but hard backward
elements: Input: base g, exponent x, modulus p
Forward (Easy): Compute y = g^x mod p using repeated squaring
Output: y
Arrow labeled 'Easy' pointing right
Backward (Hard): Given y, g, p, find x such that y = g^x mod p
Arrow labeled 'Hard (DLP)' pointing left with broken line
No efficient classical algorithm known for large p
diagrams.purpose: Visualize the one-way nature of modular exponentiation that makes Diffie-Hellman secure
